claims.claim_text: Registering a domain for the exercise costs about $12/year.
claims.anchor_text: AWS account with at least one registered domain (or willingness to register one ~$12/year)
claims.suggested_rewrite: AWS account with at least one registered domain (or willingness to register one; domain pricing varies by TLD)
claims.rationale: AWS pricing states domain pricing varies by TLD, but this exact annual figure is mutable and not directly backed on a current allowlisted page.

claims.claim_text: The October 2021 Facebook outage lasted about six hours after a routine maintenance/configuration change disrupted backbone routing and made its DNS unreachable.
claims.rationale: Meta's postmortem directly explains the backbone change and resulting DNS reachability failure, but the host is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The Facebook outage locked out 3.5 billion users, caused about $100 million in lost revenue, disrupted badge access, and coincided with a 4.9% stock drop.
claims.anchor_text: The result: 3.5 billion users locked out, an estimated $100 million in lost revenue, and employees unable to enter their own buildings because badge systems depended on internal DNS. The stock dropped 4.9% that day.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The result was a major global outage that disrupted users, affected the business, and complicated internal recovery work.
claims.rationale: This sentence bundles several precise impact claims from mixed reporting rather than one allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: Route 53 is named after DNS port 53.
claims.rationale: AWS's Route 53 features page explicitly explains the product name as a reference to DNS port 53.

claims.claim_text: Route 53 handles over a trillion DNS queries per month across AWS's global network of edge locations.
claims.anchor_text: It handles over a trillion DNS queries per month across AWS's global network of edge locations.
claims.suggested_rewrite: It is designed to handle very large query volumes across AWS's global DNS network.
claims.rationale: The exact monthly volume and edge-location framing are not stably backed on a current allowlisted page.

claims.claim_text: Standard DNS does not allow a CNAME at the zone apex, but Route 53 alias records can be used at the zone apex and alias queries to AWS resources are free.
claims.span_hint: DNS Record Types You Need to Know / ALIAS paragraph
claims.rationale: AWS docs directly compare alias and CNAME behavior, including zone-apex support and free alias queries to supported AWS resources.

claims.claim_text: When Route 53 creates a public hosted zone, it assigns four authoritative name servers across four different top-level domains.
claims.rationale: AWS documentation overview states that Route 53 populates hosted zones with four name servers across four different top-level domains.

claims.claim_text: The four-TLD spread guarantees DNS continuity even if one TLD's infrastructure has issues.
claims.anchor_text: This four-TLD spread ensures that even if one TLD's infrastructure has issues, your DNS still works.
claims.suggested_rewrite: This four-TLD spread is designed to improve availability.
claims.rationale: AWS supports the availability intent, but the specific one-TLD-failure mechanism is stronger than the cited docs justify.

claims.claim_text: Route 53 supports split-view DNS by using public and private hosted zones with the same name so internal and external queries can resolve differently.
claims.span_hint: Private Hosted Zones, paragraph after pause prompt
claims.rationale: AWS docs explicitly describe split-view DNS with public and private hosted zones of the same name.

claims.claim_text: Route 53 pricing includes $0.50 per hosted zone per month for the first 25 zones, $0.40 per million standard queries, $0.60 per million latency-based queries, and free alias A/AAAA queries to supported AWS resources.
claims.rationale: AWS pricing lists these current hosted-zone, query, and alias-query charges directly.

claims.claim_text: Health checks cost $0.50/month basic and $0.75/month for HTTPS plus string matching.
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Health checks | Pricing depends on endpoint type and optional features; check the current Route 53 pricing page |
claims.rationale: Current health-check pricing depends on AWS vs non-AWS endpoints and separately priced optional features, so this row is inaccurate.

claims.claim_text: Domain registration costs $12-40/year depending on TLD.
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Domain registration | Pricing varies by TLD and is billed in annual increments |
claims.rationale: AWS pricing says domain prices vary by TLD, but this exact range is mutable and not directly backed on one current page.

claims.claim_text: In ChangeResourceRecordSets, UPSERT creates a record if it does not exist and updates it if it does.
claims.span_hint: Basic Record Creation, paragraph after code block
claims.rationale: The Route 53 API reference explicitly defines UPSERT with this create-or-update behavior.

claims.claim_text: A weight of 0 suppresses a weighted record unless all records in the group are set to 0, in which case traffic is distributed equally.
claims.span_hint: Weighted Routing paragraph after code block; Common Mistakes table last row
claims.rationale: AWS weighted-record docs directly describe both zero-weight behaviors.

claims.claim_text: Latency-based routing uses measured latency data between query origins and AWS Regions rather than simple geographic distance.
claims.span_hint: Latency-Based Routing intro and example paragraph
claims.rationale: AWS docs explain that latency-based routing relies on latency measurements to Regions and can differ from geography.

claims.claim_text: By default, Route 53 health checks run every 30 seconds from about 15 locations worldwide.
claims.anchor_text: By default, they check your endpoint every 30 seconds from about 15 locations worldwide.
claims.suggested_rewrite: By default, health checkers run from multiple locations worldwide and can check every 30 seconds.
claims.rationale: AWS docs support the 30-second default and worldwide checker locations, but not the current-page-specific 'about 15 locations' wording.

claims.claim_text: Route 53 considers an endpoint healthy if more than 18% of health checkers report it healthy.
claims.rationale: AWS health-check docs state the 18% threshold directly.

claims.claim_text: Route 53 health checkers run outside the VPC, so private resources generally need a CloudWatch-alarm-based health check for DNS failover.
claims.span_hint: Health Check Types paragraph after table
claims.rationale: AWS docs explicitly say health checkers are outside the VPC and recommend CloudWatch-alarm-based checks for private endpoints.

claims.claim_text: DNSSEC protects against DNS spoofing by signing DNS data, and Route 53 supports DNSSEC signing for public hosted zones.
claims.span_hint: DNSSEC: Signing Your Zone, opening paragraphs
claims.rationale: AWS DNSSEC docs describe signed responses and document DNSSEC signing support for public hosted zones.

claims.claim_text: After enabling DNSSEC, you must add a DS record at the parent zone through your registrar to establish the chain of trust.
claims.span_hint: DNSSEC: Signing Your Zone, paragraph after code block
claims.rationale: AWS's DNSSEC enablement docs explicitly require establishing the chain of trust with DS records.

claims.claim_text: Route 53 has a 100% uptime SLA, uses over 200 edge locations, and has never had a complete outage since 2010.
claims.anchor_text: 1. **Route 53 has a 100% uptime SLA** -- one of the very few AWS services with this guarantee. It achieves this through a global anycast network of over 200 edge locations. AWS has never had a complete Route 53 outage since its launch in December 2010, making it one of the most reliable services in all of cloud computing.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1. **Route 53 is designed for high availability and global resilience.**
claims.rationale: This bullet mixes a real SLA detail with unsupported edge-count and outage-history claims.

claims.claim_text: The name Route 53 is also a nod to US Route 66 and an AWS naming easter egg.
claims.anchor_text: But it is also a nod to US Route 66, the famous American highway -- connecting the concept of "routing" traffic across the internet to routing cars across the country. AWS engineers love their naming easter eggs.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The name also fits the service's role in routing internet traffic.
claims.rationale: The Route 66 backstory is not backed by an allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: Route 53 processes over 1 trillion queries per month and has sub-millisecond median response time from the nearest edge location.
claims.anchor_text: 3. **Route 53 processes over 1 trillion DNS queries per month**, making it one of the largest authoritative DNS systems on Earth. Despite this scale, the median query response time is under 1 millisecond from the nearest edge location. For comparison, blinking your eye takes about 300 milliseconds.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. **Route 53 operates at very large scale** and is designed for low-latency DNS responses from a global network.
claims.rationale: The exact query-volume and latency numbers are not backed on a current allowlisted page.

claims.claim_text: Alias records were invented by AWS, later paralleled by ANAME drafts, and other providers expose similar features under different names.
claims.anchor_text: 4. **ALIAS records were invented by AWS** because standard DNS could not solve the zone-apex CNAME problem. The IETF later formalized a similar concept as the ANAME record type in RFC drafts, but as of 2026, ALIAS remains an AWS-specific extension. Other providers have their own variants: Cloudflare calls theirs "CNAME flattening" and Google Cloud DNS uses "routing records."
claims.suggested_rewrite: 4. **ALIAS records solve a practical root-domain aliasing problem in Route 53.**
claims.rationale: The invention history, ANAME-draft claim, and cross-provider comparison are not all backed by allowlisted primary sources.

claims.claim_text: Failover routing depends on health checks or evaluated target health; without them Route 53 has no endpoint health signal to drive DNS failover.
claims.span_hint: Common Mistakes table, 'No health checks on failover records'
claims.rationale: AWS failover docs explain that health checks are the mechanism Route 53 uses when choosing among failover records.

claims.claim_text: AWS publishes Route 53 health checker source IP ranges in ip-ranges.json.
claims.span_hint: Common Mistakes table, 'Health check endpoint behind security group'
claims.rationale: AWS docs explicitly direct users to ip-ranges.json for ROUTE53_HEALTHCHECKS ranges.

further_reading.title: Choosing between alias and non-alias records
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the clearest primary reference for zone-apex behavior, alias targets, and alias-query pricing.

further_reading.title: How Route 53 determines whether a health check is healthy
further_reading.why_relevant: It explains the actual health-check evaluation model, thresholds, and request intervals behind DNS failover.

further_reading.title: Configuring DNSSEC signing in Amazon Route 53
further_reading.why_relevant: It covers what DNSSEC protects against, which hosted zones support it, and how Route 53 handles signing.

notes: Ignored Mermaid diagrams, code blocks, quiz items, quiz answer text, and exercise steps per instructions. No shared section pool was used, so `source_ids` are empty and `proposed_url` carries the candidate source where applicable.
